feedback.text: From what I understand of the Digital Fairness Act, this appears to be a push toward a more just and progressive digital media landscape - an absolute necessity in the modern world, where shady practices are commonplace and little regulation exists to prevent them. I would like to bring your attention to a critical issue this initiative should address. Over the past 20 years, the video game industry has normalized numerous anti-consumer practices. One particularly egregious example is the destruction of purchased video games by disabling the servers required for them to function. Most of these games were not based on a subscription model, where customers pay a recurring fee to access the product. Instead, they were sold as one-time purchases, yet buyers were rarely informed at the point of sale that their game could eventually become unplayable. I, along with many other concerned consumers worldwide, believe this constitutes a direct violation of our consumer rights. We would be eternally grateful if this initiative could advocate for protections against the destruction of video games. The issue is so widespread that the EU Citizens Initiative "Stop Destroying Videogames" has surpassed its goal of 1 million signatures, reaching over 1.4 million as of this writing. One of the most prominent recent examples is Ubisofts decision to shut down "The Crew". The French publisher gave players just four months' notice before permanently disabling the game, leaving customers unable to play or obtain refunds for a product they had already purchased. Many other games currently face similar risks of shutdown. I sincerely hope this initiative takes action on this pressing issue, which affects millions of EU citizens and consumers worldwide.
